summary: Fresh concrete product and tool mentions from the HN front page comment crawl.
telegram_footer: 11 items surfaced | 21/21 stories scraped | history + run artifact updated

items.name: Pi
items.source_story_title: Claude Code sends 33k tokens before reading the prompt; OpenCode sends 7k
items.summary: An extensible terminal coding-agent toolkit with a deliberately small default prompt.
items.why_included: Several commenters recommended Pi as a lower-overhead, more controllable alternative to bundled coding-agent harnesses.
items.evidence: HN users said Pi sends roughly 1k tokens of setup context and praised its transparent, extensible workflow.

items.name: Dynamic Context Pruning
items.source_story_title: Claude Code sends 33k tokens before reading the prompt; OpenCode sends 7k
items.summary: An OpenCode plugin that prunes stale conversation context to reduce token use.
items.why_included: It was the clearest concrete recommendation in a discussion about agent-harness token overhead.
items.evidence: A commenter explicitly recommended it for long-horizon work, reporting substantial token savings.

items.name: Meridian
items.source_story_title: Claude Code sends 33k tokens before reading the prompt; OpenCode sends 7k
items.summary: A gateway that lets Claude Max subscriptions drive OpenCode-compatible agent clients.
items.why_included: It is a concrete routing layer for separating a coding harness from the model subscription.
items.evidence: The benchmark author identified Meridian as the local proxy used to bridge the Claude Code SDK to a standard Anthropic endpoint.

items.name: Ghostel
items.source_story_title: Ghostel.el: Terminal emulator powered by libghostty
items.summary: An Emacs terminal emulator built on libghostty-vt for responsive integrated terminals.
items.why_included: The project received detailed daily-driver praise from Emacs users and maintainers.
items.evidence: Commenters called it a game-changer, cited better responsiveness than vterm, and discussed active fixes.

items.name: Shirei
items.source_story_title: Show HN: Shirei, cross-platform GUI framework in native Go
items.summary: A native Go GUI framework with direct platform backends and immediate-mode APIs.
items.why_included: It is a new concrete framework with a practical discussion of build size and cross-compilation.
items.evidence: HN reviewers praised its native-Go approach and reported successful Linux and Windows builds.

items.name: Odin Book
items.summary: A free online book introducing the Odin systems programming language.
items.why_included: It is a focused learning resource for a concrete language, rather than a generic discussion thread.
items.evidence: The HN discussion examined Odin’s design and positioned the book as an accessible route into the language.

items.name: Croc
items.source_story_title: Croc: Securely transfer files and folders between two computers
items.summary: A command-line tool for secure, resumable peer-to-peer file and folder transfers.
items.why_included: It was directly recommended as a practical Magic Wormhole alternative.
items.evidence: A commenter reported higher throughput than Magic Wormhole from real use, citing Croc’s multiple TCP connections.

items.name: Magic Wormhole
items.source_story_title: Croc: Securely transfer files and folders between two computers
items.summary: A secure code-based tool for transferring files between computers.
items.why_included: The discussion surfaced it as the established comparison point for choosing a file-transfer tool.
items.evidence: HN commenters compared its Python implementation with Croc’s resumable transfers, proxy support, and single-binary design.

items.name: CheerpJ Applet Runner
items.source_story_title: Old and new apps, via modern coding agents
items.summary: A browser extension that runs legacy Java applets through WebAssembly.
items.why_included: It preserves old educational software without requiring a full rewrite.
items.evidence: A commenter highlighted it as a longstanding solution for running educational Java applets in modern browsers.

items.name: Landstrip
items.source_story_title: What xAI’s Grok build CLI sends to xAI: A wire-level analysis
items.summary: A cross-platform sandbox CLI for confining coding agents with explicit policies.
items.why_included: It addresses the concrete data-exposure risk raised by the Grok Build wire-level analysis.
items.evidence: A commenter recommended Landstrip as a stronger mitigation when untrusted content can influence privileged agent actions.

items.name: bubblewrap
items.source_story_title: What xAI’s Grok build CLI sends to xAI: A wire-level analysis
items.summary: A Linux sandboxing tool for isolating processes with namespaces and restricted filesystem access.
items.why_included: HN users provided a concrete, reproducible containment pattern for coding agents.
items.evidence: Commenters described using bubblewrap to hide sensitive paths, isolate networking, and allowlist only LLM-provider traffic.
